Problem 3. We say that a vector
\mathbf { u } = ( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) \in \mathbb { N } ^ { n }
if there is a non-zero vector
\mathbf { k } = ( k _ { 1 } , \ldots , k _ { n } ) \in \mathbb { Z } ^ { n }
This notion plays a very important role in many number theoretic algorithms, such
as factorisation and primality testing. It also (in a more general form) appears in
some questions in algebraic dynamics. However the algorithm to decide whether
is multiplicatively dependent is not immediately obvious. The following statement
be chosen to be reasonably small. Prove that if
\mathbf { u } = ( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) \in \mathbb { N } ^ { n }
\left\| \mathbf { u } \right\| _ { \infty } = \operatorname* { m a x } _ { 1 \leq i \leq n } \left| u _ { i } \right|
, then there is a non-zero vector
\mathbf { k } = ( k _ { 1 } , \ldots , k _ { n } ) \in \mathbb { Z } ^ { n }
it can be found in polynomial time of order

proof) which informally means that if a system of homogeneous equations with integer
coefficients has a nontrivial solution then it has an integer solutions with reasonably
small components. It is required in many applications.
